subject: Mozi Minute: Silence Creates A 32% Boost in Closing
body:
Words I like: In sales, the person who talks the first after price, loses. Sometimes the best thing to say is nothing.

Mozi Minute: The Silent Close Technique

So I stumbled on this bit of research that confirmed a long held belief of mine.

- Columbia Business School found purchases increase by 32% when salespeople extend that silence to 8+ seconds.
- Yet, research from the University of Texas shows the average salesperson waits only 2.7 seconds after asking for the sale before jumping back in.

Hence, the problem.

Theory:

Your prospect's brain needs 6-8 seconds to process a (large) decision. When you talk during that time, you interrupt their buying process. It's like hitting reset on their mental math (and increase the time to buy).

Practice:

- Ask for the sale clearly
- Shut up and count to 8 (silently)
- ...then count to 8 again

We implemented this with a sales team last month. We got a small bump in sales immediately - mainly from the guys who weren't already doing this. To be clear. The jump didn't come from changing the pitch, just from changing the pause length after asking for the sale. And the best part, there was no new training/scripting besides "count to 8 after you make the ask."

Pro tip: 8 seconds is easy to say but feels like an eternity in reality. Look at the call time on your phone or dialer to actually make sure you wait out the full length. After all, 32% higher close rates is nothing to sneeze at.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Talk less, sell more,

Alex
